exercises.title: The drop-in workbench pack
exercises.prompt: The capstone is a reusable pack you drop into any repo. Explain what the pack contains (the seven surfaces packaged), what 'cp -r and have an agent working reliably the next morning' means, and why packaging the surfaces into one directory matters.
exercises.hint: Pack: the seven workbench surfaces as files/scripts/templates/schemas in one directory — instructions router, state schemas, init script, scope contract template, feedback runner, verification gate, reviewer config, handoff template, plus an installer. cp -r drops it in; the repo now has the reliability baseline. Packaging matters: reuse, no rebuild per repo, known-good baseline.
exercises.solution:
**What the pack contains (the seven surfaces packaged):** the capstone pack is a **directory bundling all seven workbench surfaces** (Lessons 32-40) as **reusable files, scripts, templates, and schemas:**
- **Instructions** (Lessons 32-33): a **root instructions router** template + the **rule definitions** (executable constraints).
- **State** (Lesson 34): the **JSON schemas** for `agent_state.json` and `task_board.json` + the **state manager**.
- **Init** (Lesson 35): the **deterministic init script** (environment probing).
- **Scope** (Lesson 36): the **scope-contract template** + the **scope checker**.
- **Feedback** (Lesson 37): the **feedback runner** (capture real command results).
- **Verification** (Lesson 38): the **verification gate** (the deterministic done-or-not check).
- **Review + Handoff** (Lessons 39-40): the **reviewer config/rubric** + the **handoff generator**.
- **An installer script:** lays down the pack **idempotently** (next exercise).
The seven surfaces — previously built lesson by lesson — are **packaged into one drop-in directory.**

**What 'cp -r and have an agent working reliably the next morning' means:** the pack is designed so you can **`cp -r` it into any repo** (copy the directory in) and **immediately have the full workbench baseline** — the repo now has the instructions router, state schemas, init script, scope-contract template, feedback runner, verification gate, reviewer, and handoff generator **ready to use.** So **without rebuilding the workbench from scratch**, the repo is **set up for reliable agent work** — an agent working in it gets the **engineered reliability surfaces** (scope bounding, verification, durable state, feedback, review) **out of the box.** 'Have an agent working reliably the next morning' = **drop the pack in (cp -r), and the next session an agent works reliably** (with the full workbench) — no per-repo workbench-building. The pack delivers the **proven reliability** (Lesson 41's demonstrated value) **instantly to any repo.**

**Why packaging the surfaces into one directory matters:** 
- **Reuse (no rebuild per repo):** the seven surfaces are **general** (they help across repos, Lesson 41's generalization) — so **building them from scratch in every repo is wasteful and error-prone.** Packaging them into a **reusable directory** means you **build them once and reuse them everywhere** (`cp -r`) — the DRY principle (don't repeat yourself) applied to the workbench. Every new repo gets the workbench **for free** (copy the pack) instead of re-implementing it.
- **Known-good baseline:** the pack is a **pinned, tested, known-good** version of the surfaces (next exercise) — so every repo gets a **consistent, proven** workbench (not a hand-rolled, possibly-buggy one). The pack is the **standard, vetted baseline.**
- **Composability / completeness:** packaging the **seven surfaces together** ensures they **work together as a coherent whole** (they're designed to compose — state feeds verification, feedback feeds verification, etc., Lessons 38-40). A pack delivers the **complete, integrated workbench**, not scattered pieces.
- **Distribution:** a single directory is **easy to distribute, version, and update** — share it, drop it into repos, update the pack and re-install. It's the **deliverable artifact.**
Packaging matters because it turns the workbench from **'a set of techniques you rebuild each time' into 'a reusable, pinned, drop-in artifact you copy into any repo'** — making the proven reliability (Lesson 41) **instantly and consistently available everywhere.** 'The capstone is the artifact this curriculum trades on' — a reusable workbench pack, not just knowledge. The mini-track's whole point is this **reusable artifact**: eleven lessons of surfaces **compressed into a directory you `cp -r`.**

exercises.title: Pinning schemas and an idempotent installer
exercises.prompt: Explain why you pin the schemas, scripts, and templates (a known-good baseline), and implement an idempotent installer that lays down the pack. Why must the installer be idempotent, and what should it do on a repo that already has (part of) the pack?
exercises.hint: Pin: fixed, versioned schemas/scripts/templates so every repo gets the SAME known-good baseline (no drift). Installer: copy the pack files into the repo, create state files, register the init/verification scripts. Idempotent: running it twice is safe (doesn't duplicate/clobber). On a partial pack: install missing pieces, don't overwrite existing customizations (or update with care).
exercises.solution:
**Why pin the schemas, scripts, and templates (a known-good baseline):** the pack's components are **pinned** — fixed, versioned versions — so that **every repo gets the *same, known-good* baseline:**
- **Consistency / no drift:** if the schemas/scripts/templates **varied per repo** (each repo's workbench slightly different), you'd get **inconsistency and drift** — some repos with a buggy/outdated workbench, hard to reason about or support. **Pinning** ensures **every repo gets the *identical, vetted* version** — the **known-good baseline.** 
- **Reliability:** the pinned versions are **tested and proven** (Lesson 41's demonstrated value) — so every repo inherits a **reliable, working** workbench (not a hand-rolled variant that might be broken). Pinning = **a guaranteed-good starting point.**
- **Reproducibility / support:** with pinned versions, you **know exactly what workbench any repo has** (the pinned version) — reproducible, supportable, updatable (bump the pinned version, re-install). 
Pinning is the same principle as **dependency pinning / artifact pinning** (Phase 10.13's pinned artifacts, Phase 13.15's hash-pinning) — **fix the version so every consumer gets the same known-good thing**, avoiding drift and ensuring a reliable baseline. The pack's value is in being a **standard, proven, pinned baseline** — pinning is what makes it that.

**An idempotent installer:**
```python
import os, shutil

def install_pack(repo_path, pack_path):
    """Idempotently lay down the workbench pack into the repo."""
    for item in PACK_FILES:                          # the pinned pack contents
        dest = os.path.join(repo_path, item)
        if os.path.exists(dest):
            # ALREADY PRESENT: don't clobber customizations; update only if it's the pack's own
            if is_pack_managed(dest) and is_outdated(dest, pack_path):
                shutil.copy(os.path.join(pack_path, item), dest)   # update pack-managed files
            # else: leave existing (don't overwrite user customizations)
        else:
            os.makedirs(os.path.dirname(dest), exist_ok=True)
            shutil.copy(os.path.join(pack_path, item), dest)       # install missing
    # Create state files if absent (don't reset existing state)
    ensure_state_files(repo_path)
    print("workbench pack installed (idempotent)")
```
The installer **lays down the pack files** (schemas, scripts, templates) into the repo, **creating state files if absent.**

**Why the installer must be idempotent:** **idempotent** = **running it twice (or N times) is safe and has the same effect as running it once** — it must be idempotent because:
- **It will be run repeatedly:** you'll run the installer **multiple times** — to set up, to **update** the pack (re-run after a new version), or by accident. A **non-idempotent** installer would **break on the second run** — **duplicating files, clobbering state, or corrupting** the existing setup. 
- **Safety:** an idempotent installer is **safe to re-run** — it **won't duplicate, won't reset existing state, won't break** a partially-installed pack. You can run it freely (set up, update, re-run) without fear of damage. 
- **Reliability:** idempotency makes the installer **predictable and reliable** — running it always **converges to the correct installed state** (the pack present, state files intact), regardless of how many times it's run or the repo's prior state. (Same idempotency principle as Phase 13.13's safe-retry, infrastructure-as-code, package installers.)
Non-idempotent installation is a **footgun** (re-running breaks things); **idempotent installation is safe and convergent** — essential for a tool you'll run repeatedly to install and update.

**What it should do on a repo that already has (part of) the pack:** on a repo with a **partial/existing pack**, the idempotent installer should:
- **Install missing pieces** — add the pack files that **aren't there yet** (complete the installation).
- **Don't overwrite user customizations** — if a file **already exists and the user customized it** (e.g. they edited the scope-contract template or instructions for their repo), **don't clobber it** — preserve the customization. Overwriting would **destroy the user's work** (a major footgun).
- **Update pack-managed files carefully** — for files that are **the pack's own (unmodified)** and **outdated** (an older pinned version), **update them** to the new pinned version (with care/backup) — so re-running updates the pack. But **distinguish pack-managed (safe to update) from user-customized (preserve).**
- **Don't reset existing state** — if `agent_state.json` / `task_board.json` already exist (the repo has work in progress), **don't overwrite them** (that would **destroy the durable state**, Lesson 34) — only **create them if absent.**
So on a partial/existing pack, the idempotent installer: **fills in missing pieces, preserves user customizations and existing state, and updates only the pack's own outdated files (carefully)** — converging to a complete, current pack **without destroying the user's customizations or state.** It's **additive and non-destructive** — install what's missing, preserve what's customized, update the pack's own files. This makes the installer **safe to run on any repo (fresh or partial), repeatedly** — the idempotency that makes the pack a reliable, re-runnable, updatable tool.

exercises.title: The pack as the curriculum's deliverable, defending the architecture, and the workbench's lasting lesson
exercises.prompt: Explain why the reusable pack is 'the artifact this curriculum trades on', how to defend every architectural choice in the pack (each surface justified by a failure mode it prevents), the limits (the pack is a baseline, not a guarantee), and the lasting lesson of the whole agent-workbench mini-track and Phase 14.
exercises.hint: The pack is the deliverable: it turns the curriculum's knowledge into a reusable artifact that makes any repo reliable. Defend each surface by the failure mode it prevents (Lesson 26 mapping). Limits: it's a baseline (reduces but doesn't eliminate failures; needs customization, good tests, judgment). Lasting lesson: agent reliability is engineering (surfaces, verification, separation), not a model property.
exercises.solution:
**Why the reusable pack is 'the artifact this curriculum trades on':** the pack is the **culminating deliverable** — it **turns the curriculum's knowledge into a reusable, valuable artifact:**
- **Knowledge → artifact:** the whole mini-track (Lessons 31-41) **taught the surfaces and proved their value** (Lesson 41's comparison). The pack **compresses that knowledge into a directory you `cp -r`** — so the value isn't just 'you understand the workbench' but 'you have a **reusable workbench** that makes any repo reliable.' The curriculum **trades on** delivering this **concrete, reusable artifact** — not just teaching concepts but producing a **drop-in tool.**
- **It's the practical payoff:** a student finishes the mini-track with **a pack they can drop into their own repos** to get reliable agents — the **tangible, applicable outcome.** The artifact is **what makes the curriculum worth it** (knowledge + a reusable tool that delivers the proven reliability, Lesson 41).
- **It embodies the whole mini-track:** the pack **IS** the seven surfaces, the schemas, the scripts, the templates, the installer — **everything the mini-track built, packaged.** It's the **synthesis** of Lessons 31-41 into one deliverable.
'The capstone is the artifact this curriculum trades on' — the **reusable workbench pack** is the curriculum's deliverable: knowledge made into a **drop-in tool that delivers reliable agents.**

**How to defend every architectural choice in the pack (each surface justified by a failure mode):** the capstone goal is to **'defend every architectural choice'** — and you defend each surface by **the structural failure mode it prevents** (the Lesson 26 → workbench mapping):
- **Instructions as executable constraints (Lessons 32-33):** *defends against* **scope creep, forbidden actions, false 'done'** (vague prose rules ignored, Lesson 33) — rules become enforced checks.
- **Durable state (Lesson 34):** *defends against* **context loss** (Lesson 26 — the finite context wall) — state survives in the repo.
- **Init scripts (Lesson 35):** *defends against* the **cold-start tax / redundant rediscovery** — environment facts cached.
- **Scope contracts (Lesson 36):** *defends against* **scope creep** (Lesson 26) — explicit, checkable boundaries.
- **Runtime feedback (Lesson 37):** *defends against* **cascading errors** (Lesson 26 — guessing instead of seeing real results) — the agent reacts to facts.
- **Verification gates (Lesson 38):** *defends against* **task-verification failures / false 'done'** (Lesson 26) — the agent doesn't self-certify; objective verification.
- **Reviewer agent (Lesson 39):** *defends against* **the builder's blind spots** (Lesson 5's self-verification ceiling) — independent subjective review.
- **Handoff (Lesson 40):** *defends against* **context loss across sessions** (Lesson 26) — work carries across the session boundary.
**Every surface is justified by a specific failure mode it prevents** (Lesson 26's taxonomy → the workbench's surfaces) — so you can **defend each architectural choice**: 'this surface is here because it prevents *this* structural failure.' Nothing is arbitrary — **each surface is the engineered defense against a diagnosed failure mode** (Lesson 26's diagnosis → the workbench's treatment, Lesson 31). That's how you defend the architecture: **map each surface to the failure it prevents.**

**The limits (the pack is a baseline, not a guarantee):** 
- **It's a baseline, not a guarantee:** the pack **reduces but doesn't eliminate** failures — the structural failure modes are **structural** (Lesson 26 — not fully eliminable, only contained). The workbench **catches most failures** (verification, scope, feedback, review) but **can't catch everything** (e.g. a failure no check covers, a subtle bug the reviewer misses). It **dramatically improves reliability** (Lesson 41's measured outcomes) but **isn't a guarantee of correctness.**
- **Needs customization:** the pack is a **general baseline** — each repo needs **customization** (the scope contracts, the rules, the test commands are repo-specific). The pack provides the **structure and tools**; you **fill in the repo-specific content.** A dropped-in-but-uncustomized pack is less effective.
- **Depends on good underlying checks:** verification gates are only as good as the **tests** (Lesson 38's limit — weak tests → weak verification); rules only catch what they encode. The pack **enforces** your checks but **doesn't create good ones** — you still need **good tests, clear criteria, and judgment.**
- **Doesn't replace human oversight:** for high-stakes/irreversible work, **human-in-the-loop** is still needed (the pack includes approval boundaries, but humans must engage them).
- **It's an evolving baseline:** as failure modes and best practices evolve, the **pack must be updated** (the idempotent installer enables this, Exercise 2).
So the pack is a **strong, proven, reusable baseline** that **substantially improves reliability** — but it's a **baseline to build on, not a silver bullet**: it needs customization, good underlying checks, and human judgment, and it contains (doesn't eliminate) the structural failures.

**The lasting lesson of the agent-workbench mini-track and Phase 14:** the **single most important takeaway:**
- **Agent reliability is an engineering discipline, not a model property.** A **capable model is not enough** (Lesson 31) — execution reliability comes from **engineered surfaces** (instructions, state, scope, feedback, verification, review, handoff), **not from a better model.** The structural failure modes (Lesson 26) are **not fixed by capability** — they're **engineered around** (the workbench).
- **Reliability lives in the gaps — separate the builder from the verifier.** The deepest insight (Lessons 31, 38-39): **reliability comes from independent verification** — the builder can't grade itself (Lesson 5's ceiling), so you **separate building from verifying** (verification gate + reviewer agent) and **verify every output independently.** This is the course-wide theme (verifiable rewards Phase 9.12, CRITIC Lesson 5, SWE-bench Lesson 19) — **external/objective verification, not self-assessment.**
- **Ground the agent in reality, bound it, and verify it.** The surfaces all serve this: **ground** (feedback, init — real facts not guesses), **bound** (scope, constraints — limit what it does), **verify** (gates, review — confirm it's done right), **persist** (state, handoff — durable continuity). 'Capable model + engineered surfaces = reliable agent.'
- **Simple over complex, measured over hoped.** The whole phase (Lessons 12, 28, 30) — **start simple, add complexity only by measured need; evaluate everything.** The pack itself is simple files/scripts (not a complex framework), proven by measurement (Lesson 41).
The lasting lesson: **building reliable agents is an engineering discipline** — of **surfaces, separations, verification, grounding, and measurement** — **not a search for a better model or a cleverer prompt.** The **workbench pack is the embodiment** of this: a reusable artifact of **engineered reliability surfaces** that turns a capable model into a reliable agent on any repo. Phase 14 began with the agent loop (Lesson 1 — the behavior) and ends with the workbench pack (Lesson 42 — the engineering that makes the behavior *reliable*). The arc: **a capable model running a loop is an agent; a capable model running a loop *within an engineered workbench (grounded, bounded, verified, persistent)* is a *reliable* agent that ships.** That engineering — not the model — is agent engineering, and the pack is its deliverable. **Reliability is engineered, not given** — the lasting lesson of the entire phase.
